recent_candidates.title: Playbook Special Edition: Meet Our New Authors
recent_candidates.excerpt: The unofficial guide to official Washington. Jul 19, 2026 View in browser By Adam Wren, Dasha Burns and Will Steakin Big news this evening: Playbook is getting a new team. Beginning tomorrow, Playbook will be co-authored by ADAM WREN, DASHA BURNS and WILL STEAKIN — three of the sharpest reporters in American politics, taking the wheel at exactly the moment when stakes are the highest. A midterm year full of surprises, from President Donald Trump’s endorsements to a nationwide left-wing challenge toppling establishment Democrats. A wide-open 2028 race already taking shape. And a battle over the future of both parties that grows more intense by the day. They’ll be working over the next several weeks toward an official relaunch on August 31, when you can expect a newsletter with more reporting, deeper sourcing and an even sharper read on where power is shifting — from the campaign trail to Capitol Hill to the West Wing. What won't change: Playbook will still be the indispensable morning briefing you start your day with. The Playbook Podcast is also getting a refresh — anchored by DASHA, with a rotating cast of her fellow authors and POLITICO journalists breaking down the biggest stori

recent_candidates.title: Iran’s Southern Front
recent_candidates.excerpt: Today in The New York Review of Books: Amir Ahmadi Arian focuses on the south of Iran, currently bearing the brunt of Trump’s war; Aryeh Neier defends the International Criminal Court; Geoffrey O’Brien watches Spielberg’s latest; and, from the archives, Geoffrey O’Brien on Minority Report. Amir Ahmadi Arian The War of the South [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/19/the-war-of-the-south-iran/] For most of its modern history, the south of Iran has been subject to two forms of colonialism, one from the land and the other from the sea. Aryeh Neier ‘So-Called International Law’ [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/18/so-called-international-law-trump-icc/] The US has long held the International Criminal Court in suspicion—but never before has an administration tried to destroy it altogether. Geoffrey O’Brien They Knew Too Much [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/14/they-knew-too-much-disclosure-day/] Steven Spielberg’s new film is a discordant, ambiguous errand into the maze of ufology. Free from the Archives This week in the NYR Online, Geoffrey O’Brien reviews Steven Spielberg’s latest [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/14/they-knew-too-much-disclosure-day/] science f

recent_candidates.title: Who Cares about the Odyssey?
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://www.raweggstack.com/p/who-cares-about-the-odyssey I promised myself I would not write about The Odyssey. “Charles,” that little voice in my head began to say, “there is absolutely nothing of value to be written about that cursed film, certainly not in an opinion piece. Enough ink has already been spilled, enough Tweets have already been Tweeted, including paid sponsorships dispensed from a hasbara fund so deep it would make even our man in Tel Aviv swoon from vertigo. Just leave it be.” After a brief pause, the homunculus piloting my meat-suit continued: “Remember: You’ve posted the hilarious memes and called her Helen of Detroit—for which you received furious copyright strikes from the film studio—and you’ve also offered compelling analysis of why the film is not a ‘Trojan Horse” expertly crafted to smuggle an anti-woke message into the very heart of Hollywood’s wokest of wokes. People can figure out for themselves that a guy like Jonathan Pageau—the sort of theory-obsessed blowhard who can’t read a menu without pontificating about the symbolism of the entrees in Leviticus—is just talking out of his French-Canadian trou du cul. (Yes, you could

recent_candidates.title: Ep. 4: In the World, Not of It
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://catholicmanhood.substack.com/p/ep-4-in-the-world-not-of-it What does it actually mean to be in the world but not of it? In this meditation, Nick walks through the Letter to Diognetus, a second-century document written to a pagan who wanted to understand what Christians were made of. The portrait it paints is of men fully present in the world and made of completely different material. Citizens who were also aliens. Men whose lives required an explanation. The question it puts to every Catholic man: Does your life require an explanation? Companion Article In the World. Not of It. — [ https://substack.com/redirect/4fa714d1-618e-40ce-a08d-cab891452cb9?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ]catholicmanhood.substack.com [ https://substack.com/redirect/9f8e539e-e4e0-4f09-807d-cd1790491e84?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ] Credits Host: Nick Sledge Producer: John Osterhoudt Follow Catholic Manhood on Social Media X [ https://substack.com/redirect/f1aabe0c-0bd1-49e4-a6c5-a1aa04a65eae?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ] FB [ https://substack.com/redirect/7be53267-2c

recent_candidates.title: New Article: Wiring Capital to Compute
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://letter.palladiummag.com/p/new-article-wiring-capital-to-compute This article by Daniel Painter was published at Palladium Magazine [ https://substack.com/redirect/2c620a62-b931-4a27-8482-4f3b72347c26?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ] on July 20, 2026. Over a thousand acres of land in Abilene, Texas, lie flattened with graded earth and poured concrete. The foundation pits are filled with conduit bundles and switchgear, and in some quadrants, fully operational substations have been erected. However, other quadrants sit empty, waiting on a schedule that keeps sliding to the right. Although the supercomputer intended for the site is humming with activity, what registers to a visitor is that the campus is only half-alive. Abilene was the intended flagship of the Stargate consortium. Announced from the White House days after President Trump’s 2025 inauguration, SoftBank’s Masayoshi Son, Oracle’s Larry Ellison, and OpenAI’s Sam Altman stood beside the president as he declared the project’s ambition to pour $500 billion into a combined data center and supercomputer over a four-year [ https://substack.com/redirect/a972555

recent_candidates.title: Short Take: Was “The Odyssey” about Embezzlement?
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://www.anarchonomicon.com/p/short-take-was-the-odyssey-about Was “The Odyssey” about Embezzlement? HELLO. This is insane. So Nolan is getting 15% off direct Revenue, (Not profits, Gross) and his wife another 5%) from the Odyssey, basically risk free, AFTER his 20 million up front. That’s a better cut than a lot of mob bosses take off drug dealers. (which tend to vary between 5 and 30% depending on how much violence they can command) And probably core stars (Daiman and Hathaway mostly) are getting some smaller percent of the same deal. This is unheard of. Basically a violation of fiduciary duties to the investors. Studio execs paid all these people to make this movie this way against their own investors interests. So if another 10-30% of Gross is divided across Daiman, Hathaway, Holand, Pattinson, and Theron... You know the real stars (Zendaya, Nyong’o, and Page don’t draw crowds and aren’t getting that)... Then you might have to effectively cut all revenue first by 50% for theaters, then by another 40-50% for the star power. It other words... The Odyssey would need to make 800million- 1 billion before it could even hypothetically start to turn a pr

Ahead of his first visit to the White House, Lebanese President Joseph Aoun has clearly recognized this new reality. “It is my duty to seize all opportunities that come to us with solutions,” Aoun said [https://www.nidaalwatan.com/article/390090-%D8%B9%D9%88%D9%86-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B3%D9%84%D8%B7%D8%A9-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B3%D9%8A%D8%A7%D8%B3%D9%8A%D8%A9-%D9%88%D9%82%D8%A7%D8%A6%D8%AF-%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%AC%D9%8A%D8%B4-%D8%B9%D9%84%D9%89-%D9%82%D9%84%D8%A8-%D9%88%D8%A7%D8%AD%D8%AF] in a July 11 interview. “And today there is a golden opportunity called America that we must seize.” The

recent_candidates.title: Were There Many Nonwhites in Bronze Age Greece?
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://www.stevesailer.net/p/were-there-many-nonwhites-in-bronze A curious feature of Christopher Nolan’s hit movie The Odyssey is that Bronze Age Greece in 1200 BC is portrayed as having about the same racial demographics as an upscale Los Angeles private school in 2026 where Nolan would send his kids: mostly white and the rest a random mixture of East Asians, South Asians, Sub-Saharans, and even one Latino. Demographically, the cast of The Odyssey is much like that seen in a high school musical production of My Fair Lady. (It even has a girl playing a boy.) Likewise, the casting of The Odyssey seems slightly amateurish. It’s not at all uncommon in adventure epics to portray characters from far away, such as T.E. Lawrence in Lawrence of Arabia, Gurkha soldier Billy Fish in The Man Who Would Be King, and Azeem in the Kevin Costner Robin Hood. But these kind of movies traditionally took pains to offer an explanation for why, say, Morgan Freeman is in medieval Sherwood Forest. The backstories of how they got there are part of the adventure. But The Odyssey is very different in that it doesn’t deign to offer any explanations for its distracting casting. L

recent_candidates.title: Corruption Takes Center Stage in Ukraine’s National Conversation
recent_candidates.excerpt: Welcome to The Ukraine Compass, a weekly digest of Ukrainian commentary and analysis from across the political spectrum only for War on the Rocks members. Each Monday, we bring you a curated selection of articles from Ukrainian media offering insight into how Ukrainians themselves debate the issues shaping their country.American coverage often narrows the view to the battlefield — these pieces widen it, revealing the texture of daily life, politics, and public argument in a nation at war. The perspectives gathered here are varied, candid, and often surprising, together forming a more complete picture of Ukraine as it really is.Frontline and StrategyУкраїнська правда The post Corruption Takes Center Stage in Ukraine’s National Conversation appeared first on War on the Rocks .

The proposal will likely be approved by Taiwanese President Lai Ching-te, whose party joined in proposing [https://www.taiwannews.com.tw/news/6400549] the measure as part of a multi-party legislative committee. recent_candidates.title: Why Are Whites Hiding Their Racial Superiority?
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://www.stevesailer.net/p/why-are-whites-hiding-their-racial Is race a social construct? Or is it a good idea? From the New York Times science section: This Lifesaving Genetics Tool Works Best if You’re White [ https://substack.com/redirect/ff29e0fe-c7fb-4018-9cf6-763098a32c9d?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ] Genetic prediction models are poised to revolutionize medicine. But because they have been trained on European DNA, they threaten to widen health care disparities. By Emily Baumgaertner Nunn July 20, 2026 The National Institutes of Health recently announced a remarkable achievement: the world’s largest database of human genomes, a repository of information that the agency says will help usher in a new era in personalized medicine. Scientists are already developing clinical tools and techniques based on genomic information gathered from the agency’s All of Us program. One of the most promising? Polygenic risk scores. These can help predict a person’s likelihood of developing complex diseases, like cardiovascular disease and breast cancer, from as early as infancy. But there’s a glaring problem: The tools were tr

recent_candidates.title: The DNC Is MIA
recent_candidates.excerpt: Back in May, the Democratic National Committee belatedly and reluctantly released its autopsy of the 2024 election. The report itself was, Daniel Schlozman writes this week in the NYR Online [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/20/the-dnc-against-itself/], a mess, “full of truisms and potted histories,” while its publication—from conception down through the DNC’s efforts to suppress it—invited criticism from leftists and centrists alike. Indeed, he continues, “It is probably fair to say that the rising forces in today’s party, from Abundance-pilled moderates to socialists, view the DNC as much less central than their predecessors did.” Schlozman tells the history of the DNC, from before the Civil War to today, and makes the case that it has been weakening ever since the end of the Obama administration, giving way to a web of ultrarich donors and various unaccountable PACs (lately empowered by the Supreme Court to raise and spend more money than ever). And while there is much to criticize about the committee, Schlozman argues that we may miss if it drifts too far out of relevance: “A heroic party is hard to imagine at this juncture, but it’s still worth trying to build a competen

recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail19.com/t/r-e-tutkhudt-oophjwr-e/ July 21, 2026 | The Dispatch The Unrelenting Reality of the Iran War [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/21/the-unrelenting-reality-of-the-iran-war/] This is probably Trump’s last chance to show why the regime should fear him. Reuel Marc Gerecht [https://www.fdd.org/team/reuel-marc-gerecht/] | Resident Scholar A U.S. Marine from the 11th Marine Expeditionary Unit aboard M/T Wen Yao in the Gulf of Oman, on July 16, 2026. (U.S. Central Command via X) Excerpt Since the Islamic Revolution in 1979, the Iranian regime has never encountered an American president like Donald Trump: He exaggerates, deceives, and prevaricates as much as it does. Indeed, Trump might win a mendacity contest since a swath of Iran’s Islamic revolutionaries—those who are more in the mold of founding father Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini—prefer to vent veraciously since they are men of modern Islamic principles and pride. With Iran and the U.S. again exchanging barrages, Trump may hew a bit closer to the truth, which Iranians, who never wanted a ceasefire as much as the president, largely define. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt

recent_candidates.title: Rosalind Belben’s singularly ingenious novel ‘Is Beauty Good’
recent_candidates.excerpt: “Belben has written pages about sexual desire, frustration and loss which are clearer and more compelling than any I can think of in literature. She has a photographic eye for natural beauty, and is also tough-minded and funny.” —Maggie Gee, The Observer IS BEAUTY GOOD [https://www.nyrb.com/products/is-beauty-good]Rosalind Belben Introduction by Esther KinskyIs Beauty Good is a novel populated with voices, each musing, in its own way, on the titular question. In thirty short, self-contained chapters set in places as varied as Berlin trams, English gardens, and the tops of mountains, people talk to silent listeners, engage in soliloquy, or else write down their meditations for a deaf man. Elegiac and ironic, the novel speaks most hauntingly of the loss of the natural world, which is of course related to the loss of all those senses and faculties of appreciation that make us feel alive. The sheer sensuous force of Rosalind Belben’s prose is on full display in this work of uncommon inventiveness and philosophical potency, which recalls the fictions of Virginia Woolf and Thomas Bernhard. BUY THE BOOK [https://www.nyrb.com/products/is-beauty-good] For three days only, Is Beauty Good is

(Mustafa Hassona/Anadolu Agency/Getty Images) A Texan’s time on the run came to an end this month when the FBI extradited [https://x.com/FBIDallas/status/2077037975007826390] him from Qatar to the United States. FBI Dallas coordinated with Qatari authorities to apprehend the suspect, a 28-year-old man allegedly involved in a sweeping money laundering network who has evaded justice since 2021. The story is an example of what the U.S.-Qatar partnership can achieve when it’s working as it should. However, the success does more to accentuate the partnership’s shortcomings than add a notch in its belt. recent_candidates.title: China’s Support for Russia’s Arctic Energy Buildout Complicated by Sanctions — But Not Prevented
recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail19.com/t/r-e-tutukdtt-oophjwr-ju/ July 23, 2026 | Op-ed China’s Support for Russia’s Arctic Energy Buildout Complicated by Sanctions — But Not Prevented [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/23/chinas-support-for-russias-arctic-energy-buildout-complicated-by-sanctions-but-not-prevented/] Max Meizlish [https://www.fdd.org/team/max-meizlish/] | CEFP Research Fellow Pipelines leading to the Bovanenkovo gas field on the Yamal peninsula in the Arctic circle on May 21, 2019. (Alexander NEMENOV/AFP via Getty Images) China and Russia purport to have a “no limits” partnership, but recent developments in the Arctic suggest otherwise. Citing a Russian government presentation originally reported by Russia’s Vedomosti newspaper, the Kyiv Post reported [https://www.kyivpost.com/post/80572] on July 18 that China would only supply specialized propulsion and steering systems for Russia’s large ice-class vessels if the United States eased sanctions. Moscow cannot produce these components domestically, and Western firms will not supply them. Beijing also seems unwilling, though its support for other parts of Russia’s heavily sanctioned Arctic energy sector r

recent_candidates.title: Ukraine’s defense leadership crisis becomes blessing in disguise
recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail19.com/t/r-e-tutukjjy-oophjwr-dh/ July 23, 2026 | FDD's Long War Journal Ukraine’s defense leadership crisis becomes blessing in disguise [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/23/ukraines-defense-leadership-crisis-becomes-blessing-in-disguise/] John Hardie [https://www.fdd.org/team/john-hardie/] | Russia Program Deputy Director Yevhenii Khmara, Andrii Hnatov, Oleksandr Syrskyi, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ihor Skybiuk, and Pavlo Palisa. (President of Ukraine via X) “You can always count on the Americans to do the right thing after they’ve tried everything else,” goes an aphorism often apocryphally attributed to Winston Churchill. Much the same can be said for the latest shakeup in Ukraine’s defense leadership. Bowing to a demand from protests unleashed by his short-sighted decision to fire Ukraine’s defense minister, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has appointed Major General Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, to replace the controversial General Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief. Drapatyi is widely respected and part of a new generation of officers molded by war with Russia rather than the Soviet system. However, he enters his new rol

The official threatened legal action against them. The crackdown comes as social media has fueled backlash over policies increasing China’s role in the Kyrgyz economy. recent_candidates.title: China Is Helping Erdogan Build a Surveillance State
recent_candidates.excerpt: No Images? Click here https://fdd-new.cmail20.com/t/r-e-tutuhlkk-oophjwr-ji/ July 23, 2026 | Real Clear World China Is Helping Erdogan Build a Surveillance State [https://www.fdd.org/analysis/2026/07/23/china-is-helping-erdogan-build-a-surveillance-state/] Tyler Stapleton | Director of Congressional Relations at FDD Action Sinan Ciddi [https://www.fdd.org/team/sinan-ciddi/] | Turkey Program Director and Senior Fellow Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and Chinese President Xi Jinping in Tianjin, China on August 31, 2025. (Turkish Presidency/Murat Cetinmuhurdar/Handout/Anadolu via Getty Images) Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan is no stranger to the machinery of repression. He has throttled internet providers, monitored political opponents, and folded once-independent media into a loyalist chorus. Now he is turning to China to tighten his grip on the information space—and Washington and Brussels should take notice. Chinese telecom giants have quietly become fixtures of Turkey’s digital backbone. ZTE and Huawei hold [https://muse.jhu.edu/article/991333] large, and in some cases controlling, stakes in Netaş, Turkcell, Türk Telekom, and Vodafone Turkey—the country’s four large

recent_candidates.source: The New York Review of Books
recent_candidates.title: Poisoned Earth
recent_candidates.excerpt: Today in The New York Review of Books: Helen Epstein assesses the toxic pollution Israel has left in Gaza; Sophia Goodfriend looks at Israel’s use of AI in its subjugation of Palestinians; Anthony Roth Costanzo reads an essay by Daniel Mendelsohn for the latest episode of our podcast Private Life; Merve Emre interviews two interviewers; and, from the archives, Brad Leithauser on Junichiro Tanizaki. Helen Epstein Gaza’s Deadly Soil [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/22/gazas-deadly-soil/] Amid tight Israeli restrictions, new remote research is starting to help us understand the scale of toxic contamination across the Gaza Strip. Sophia Goodfriend Target Practice [https://www.nybooks.com/online/2026/07/23/target-practice-israel-artificial-intelligence/] Artificial intelligence systems—now an integral part of the Israeli war machine—began as tools developed to surveil every aspect of Palestinian life. Private Life: A New York Review Podcast This episode features a reading of Daniel Mendelsohn’s review of the film Troy [https://www.nybooks.com/articles/2004/06/24/a-little-iliad/], an adaptation of the Iliad, from the Review’s June 24, 2004, issue. It is read by the countertenor an

recent_candidates.title: Dirigibles
recent_candidates.excerpt: View this post on the web at https://antelopehillpublishing.substack.com/p/dirigibles Crisscrossed again, the golden, gloaming sky, in the eye of my mind, by dirigibles, knowing, in some grander timeline, still glide those silent giants, indivisible from flight, itself. What cunning of reason once filled the wind with titans from Rio, to Frankfurt, to Manhattan? What treason, what envy, cast them down? From far below, I chart their course—through hardships, to the stars. Per aspera ad astra. Every myth shall fill its form. And from the abattoirs of history, a ship reforged will drift. Ironclad orcas, shining in the sun, halfway between what was and what’s to come. Alexander Ross is a guest contributor who has been featured in our writing competition books. Follow him for more here [ https://substack.com/redirect/43126080-59c9-43d2-a964-02a55f3e5162?j=eyJ1IjoiMmpwdzcifQ.3xfUjhbxmXCecLGnlb_rhBXs-3STnTomTjes1rmBMMI ]. Thanks for reading Antelope Hill Publishing! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support our work. Paid subscribers have access to an additional podcast episode each month. Unsubscribe https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9hbnRlbG9wZWhpbGxwdWJsaXNoaW

recent_candidates.title: The King of Cool: Mobile Refrigeration and the Remaking of Global Logistics
recent_candidates.excerpt: Editor’s note: This is the fifth article in a limited series celebrating American defense technologies born from wartime and their effects on broader national security, politics, and society. This series will run for several weeks to commemorate America’s 250th anniversary, and winners will be selected by a reader vote undertaken through our newsletter later this summer. Prior installments can be found at the Arsenal of Innovation page.Have you ever wondered how the United States transitioned from only having local fresh produce to an abundance of perishable goods available year-round across the country, even during the off-season? recent_candidates.title: The Importance of the Invasion of Sicily
recent_candidates.excerpt: On July 10, 1943, the Allied invasion of Sicily — codenamed Operation Husky — put more assault troops ashore on its first day than the Normandy landings would 11 months later. Despite the scale of these amphibious and vertical assaults on the Mediterranean’s largest island, the images most carry of this campaign come from a movie: Patton. In the 1970 film, U.S. Lt. Gen. George S. Patton wins the race to Messina and waits, smirking, as British Lt. Gen. Bernard Montgomery’s column rolls in late. The scene is an invention that reduces the campaign to a duel between two generals’ The post The Importance of the Invasion of Sicily appeared first on War on the Rocks .
